results.organization: Italian Agency for Development Cooperation (AICS)
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations
organization_type: Economic operators (companies/firms) registered on the DGCS evaluators list

application_process.description: Submission of expression of interest (manifestazione di interesse) via email or PEC to niamey@pec.aics.gov.it by 12:00 CET

eligible_activities: Development of an integrated monitoring system for data collection and analysis
Support and training for AICS staff on monitoring system management
Completion and validation of baseline data and indicators
Intermediate and final evaluation of OSC-managed projects under FASENDA and RESTA initiatives
Data collection, activity progress analysis, recommendations formulation
funding_restrictions: Maximum contract value is €210,000 (net of applicable local taxes); this is an indicative ceiling only
Not a contractual offer or formal tender initiation; preliminary market survey only
Minimum 5 operators will be invited; maximum 5 selected from valid candidacies
results.proposal_summary: The Italian Agency for Development Cooperation (AICS), Niamey office, issued a market survey notice to identify economic operators for procurement of monitoring, indicator measurement, and intermediate/final evaluation services for the RESTA (Niger, AID 013138/01/0) and FASENDA (Chad, AID 013128) initiatives. The maximum estimated contract value is €210,000 over 36 months (renewable). Eligible applicants must be economic operators registered on the DGCS list of recognised evaluators, with annual turnover of at least €350,000 and relevant prior experience. The submission deadline was 18 March 2026 at 12:00 CET via email/PEC to niamey@pec.aics.gov.it. This is a service procurement (not a grant), and the deadline has passed, rendering the call inactive.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the initiatives (RESTA, FASENDA) relate to development cooperation in Niger and Chad and may touch on livelihood/decent work themes, the call is a service procurement for monitoring and evaluation consultancy with no alignment to ILO's mandate or role as a grant recipient.

results.exclusion_reason: Not an open call with an active application window: the page is a programme description page for the RICH initiative, not a specific CfP with a submission deadline or open application round.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The page references a 'First Round of Innovative Solutions Grants' already awarded to selected cities, suggesting grants exist, but no active grant window is currently described.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No funding amounts or grant size ranges are specified on this page.

Deadline is future.evidence: No submission deadline is provided anywhere on the page; the call for proposals section only asks users to sign up for a mailing list to be notified of future calls.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page is a programme description for RICH, not a specific open CfP. The text explicitly states 'Sign up for the mailing list to hear about calls for proposals,' indicating no active application window exists.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The primary focus is disability-inclusive urban resilience and accessible city infrastructure, which falls outside the ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, social protection, and social dialogue.

results.title: Resilient and Inclusive Cities Hub (RICH) – Call for Proposals
results.organization: Global Disability Fund (GDF) / German Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development (BMZ)
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations

application_process.description: Sign up for mailing list to be notified of future calls for proposals

eligible_activities: Piloting inclusive urban resilience solutions
Accessibility gap assessments in infrastructure, services, and policies
Participatory data collection and AI integration in urban planning
Inclusive disaster preparedness initiatives
Integration of georeferenced disability data into city planning
Development of national disability registries
Accessibility improvements in public housing and educational facilities
results.proposal_summary: The Resilient and Inclusive Cities Hub (RICH) is a programme hosted by the Global Disability Fund (GDF) and seeded by the German Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development (BMZ), aimed at creating a global network of disability-inclusive cities. The programme focuses on accessible, equitable, and resilient urban development for persons with disabilities, aligning with SDG 10 and SDG 11. While a first round of innovative solutions grants has already been awarded to cities in India, Cambodia, Nigeria, Sierra Leone, and Egypt, and large-scale acceleration programmes are underway in Kenya, Peru, Philippines, Jordan, Mali, and Ethiopia, no active call for proposals is currently open. Prospective applicants are directed to sign up for a mailing list to be notified of future funding rounds. No submission deadline, grant size, or eligibility criteria for an active call are specified.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The RICH programme focuses primarily on disability-inclusive urban resilience and accessible infrastructure, with only peripheral overlap with the ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment policy, social protection, and social dialogue.

results.exclusion_reason: Funding objective is investment mobilisation (blue bonds, private capital, blended finance) rather than ILO-implementable programmatic activity; also not a grant instrument; no submission deadline present; not a standard open Call for Proposals.
results.classification_summary: The Cabo Verde Blu-X Facility 2.0 is a blended-finance investment vehicle targeting $50M in blue economy and tourism capital, offering a 4%+ coupon yield on blue bonds — a financial return instrument, not a non-reimbursable grant. The page is an investor-facing pitch seeking DFIs, ESG investors, and diaspora capital, not an open Call for Proposals with a submission deadline. The funding objective centres on mobilising private capital for ocean economy investment, which falls outside ILO-implementable technical assistance or policy work. The call is therefore ineligible on multiple grounds: it is not a grant, there is no application deadline, it is not an open CfP, and the funding objective is private-sector investment mobilisation.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The page targets institutional ESG investors, DFIs, impact investors, sovereign wealth funds, and diaspora networks as eligible participants — no mention of UN agencies or intergovernmental organisations as eligible applicant types.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The facility offers a 4%+ coupon yield on blue bonds, which is a debt/return instrument. No non-reimbursable grant component is described.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No grant instrument exists; the $50M figure refers to the investment fund target, not a grant award.

Deadline is future.evidence: No submission deadline of any kind is stated anywhere on the page.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page is an investor pitch/prospectus, not a Call for Proposals. There is no application form, submission deadline, or funding cycle identifier. It is one of ten opportunities listed on the invest.jointsdgfund.org aggregator site.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The primary purpose is mobilisation of private capital through blue bonds and blended finance to develop Cabo Verde's blue economy — a private-sector investment vehicle. This is not technical assistance, capacity building, or policy work that the ILO would implement.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: No procurement or tender language found; the page is an investment opportunity pitch.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Not applicable; no grant co-financing requirement stated.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No accreditation requirement mentioned.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium or partnership leadership rules described.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: No geographic restriction on investor origin is stated.

results.title: Cabo Verde Blu-X Facility 2.0
results.organization: Joint SDG Fund
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies
eligible_activities: Blue economy development (ocean innovation, marine conservation finance)
Sustainable tourism development (eco-experiences)
Ocean technology development
Climate-resilient livelihood creation for coastal communities
Marine conservation finance
results.proposal_summary: The Cabo Verde Blu-X Facility 2.0 is a $50M blended-finance investment vehicle managed under the Joint SDG Fund's invest platform, aimed at catalysing over $200M in public and private investment to transform Cabo Verde into Africa's leading blue economy hub. The facility offers a 4%+ coupon yield on blue bonds and targets institutional ESG investors, DFIs, impact investors, sovereign wealth funds, and diaspora networks over a 3–5 year investment period. It is focused on ocean innovation, sustainable tourism, marine conservation, and coastal community livelihoods in Cabo Verde, with an ambition to serve as a replicable model for other Small Island Development States. The page presents an investor pitch rather than an open Call for Proposals — there is no submission deadline, no grant instrument, and the primary mechanism is private capital mobilisation through blue bonds.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the facility mentions creating livelihoods for coastal communities, its primary purpose is private capital mobilisation through blue bonds — far outside the ILO's mandate of technical assistance on labour standards, decent work, and social protection.

results.exclusion_reason: Funding objective is investment mobilisation / blended finance, not ILO-implementable technical assistance; no open Call for Proposals with a submission deadline; funding instrument involves equity/credit returns (IRR 12-18%), not a grant.
results.classification_summary: This page describes a blended-finance investment vehicle seeking private and institutional capital to deploy $65M in Zanzibar's seaweed/blue economy sector, with projected financial returns of 6–18% IRR. The primary purpose is mobilising private investment capital, not funding technical assistance or programmatic work of the type ILO delivers. No grant instrument, no submission deadline, and no open call for proposals are present — the page is an investor-outreach landing page soliciting co-investors.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The page explicitly solicits 'investors' — DFIs, impact investors, private equity, corporate investors — as the target applicant/partner category. UN agencies or IGOs are not listed as eligible applicants; the framing is exclusively financial/investment-oriented.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The fund targets 'blended returns: 6-8% on credit guarantee schemes with 12-18% IRR on seaweed processing and export ventures.' This is an investment/equity vehicle with expected financial returns, not a non-reimbursable grant.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No grant component exists; the $65M figure represents an investment fund target with return expectations, not a grant amount.

Deadline is future.evidence: No submission deadline of any kind is stated anywhere on the page. There is no application window or call closing date.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: This is an investor-outreach landing page within a multi-opportunity aggregator (Joint SDG Fund invest portal). It describes an investment facility with no specific open call, no application form, no submission deadline, and no defined funding round identifier.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The stated objective is to 'deploy $65M' and 'unlock $200M+ in Blue Economy Investment' through a blended-finance vehicle offering IRR returns. The call centres on mobilising private capital and investment into commercial seaweed processing and export ventures — not technical assistance, policy work, or capacity building.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: Not a procurement or consultancy tender; it is an investment opportunity pitch.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Co-investment is the fundamental structure of the vehicle, but there is no grant application process to which a co-financing requirement would apply.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No accreditation requirement is mentioned.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are stated.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: No geographic restriction on investor origin is stated.

results.title: Zanzibar Blue & Green Economy Transformation Fund
results.organization: Joint SDG Fund
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies
eligible_activities: Scaling seaweed production across coastal villages
Agricultural credit for seaweed farming households
Climate-resilient marine agriculture infrastructure
Reducing post-harvest losses
Value-addition through processing
Credit guarantee schemes
Seaweed processing and export ventures
results.proposal_summary: The Zanzibar Blue & Green Economy Transformation Fund is a Joint SDG Fund blended-finance investment vehicle targeting $65M in deployable capital to establish Zanzibar (Tanzania) as the Indian Ocean's leading seaweed economy, seeking to catalyse over $200M in additional public and private investment. The fund targets DFIs, impact investors, private equity, and corporate co-investors, offering blended returns of 6–8% on credit guarantee schemes and 12–18% IRR on seaweed processing/export ventures over a 4–5 year period. There is no grant component, no open call for proposals, and no submission deadline; the page functions as an investor-outreach landing page. The ILO is not among the eligible applicant categories and the fund's primary purpose — mobilising private investment capital — falls outside ILO's implementing mandate.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the fund touches on women's economic empowerment and sustainable livelihoods for coastal communities, its primary purpose is private investment mobilisation in a commercial blue economy venture, with no linkage to labour standards, social protection, or decent work programming of the type ILO delivers.

results.exclusion_reason: Funding objective is not ILO-implementable: the facility is a blended finance/investment vehicle targeting private capital mobilisation, thematic bond issuance, and venture capital deployment, not a grant for technical assistance or programmatic work. Additionally, no grant instrument is present; the facility offers financial returns (6-8% yield) and leverage multiples, indicating investment capital rather than non-reimbursable grants. No submission deadline is identified, so the call is not an open grant window.
results.classification_summary: The ASSIST Facility 2.0 is a blended finance investment vehicle seeking to mobilise $150M to catalyse $10B in sustainable impact financing in Indonesia, offering 6-8% financial returns and leverage multiples through thematic bonds and venture capital. This is not a grant instrument — it creates financial returns/obligations, not non-reimbursable contributions. The facility's primary purpose is private capital mobilisation and investment, not programmatic technical assistance deliverable by the ILO. No application deadline or open call for proposals is identified.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The page targets investors (sovereign wealth funds, DFIs, institutional ESG investors, corporate impact investors, Islamic finance institutions), not UN agencies or implementing partners. Eligible 'applicants' are financial investors, not intergovernmental organisations seeking grants.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The facility explicitly offers '6-8% yield with between 10:1 and 15:1 leverage multiplier' and targets investment capital mobilisation through thematic bonds and venture capital deployment — not non-reimbursable grant funding.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No grant instrument exists; the $150M figure refers to investment capital, not grant funding.

Deadline is future.evidence: No submission deadline or application window is stated anywhere on the page.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page is an investment opportunity pitch ('To invest contact...') with no call for proposals, no application form, no submission deadline, and no defined funding round. It is an investment solicitation page, not a grant CfP.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The facility's central objective is mobilising private capital and investment through blended finance mechanisms, thematic bond issuance, and venture capital deployment to unlock $10B in impact financing. This is a financial intermediation vehicle, not a technical assistance or programmatic grant aligned with ILO implementation mandates.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: No procurement or tender language; this is an investment opportunity solicitation.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No co-financing requirement stated; the facility itself seeks investors to co-invest alongside the fund.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No accreditation requirement mentioned.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules mentioned.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Potential partners listed include global investors (ASEAN+3, Indonesian diaspora networks, international DFIs), suggesting no geographic restriction on investor origin.

results.title: Accelerate SDGs Investments in Indonesia (ASSIST) Facility 2.0
results.organization: Joint SDG Fund

results.proposal_summary: The ASSIST Facility 2.0, managed by the Joint SDG Fund, is a $150M blended finance investment vehicle for Indonesia targeting the mobilisation of $10B in additional public and private sustainable impact financing by 2030. The facility offers investors 6-8% financial returns with 10:1–15:1 leverage through thematic bond issuance and venture capital deployment, with a 5-year duration. It focuses on scaling Islamic sustainable finance, supporting 500,000+ MSMEs, and bridging Indonesia's SDG financing gap. Target investors include sovereign wealth funds, DFIs, institutional ESG investors, and Islamic finance institutions. There is no grant instrument, no open call for proposals, and no submission deadline — the facility is an investment solicitation, not a CfP eligible for ILO participation.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the facility references MSME livelihoods and SDG financing gaps relevant to decent work outcomes, its primary mechanism is private capital mobilisation and financial investment intermediation, which falls outside ILO's implementing mandate for technical assistance and labour standards work.

results.exclusion_reason: Funding objective is not ILO-implementable: this is a private investment mobilisation vehicle targeting blended finance, equity/IRR returns, and private capital for commercial agro-processing; not a grant. Additionally, no grant instrument is offered and no submission deadline exists.

The ILO is not an appropriate recipient as the vehicle is designed for financial intermediaries and private capital, not programmatic technical assistance.

Deadline is future.evidence: No submission deadline of any kind is provided on the page.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The central objective is mobilising USD 180M+ in private investment with financial returns; it is a blended-finance/investment vehicle for private capital, not a grant for technical assistance or programmatic decent work activities.

results.proposal_summary: The Nepal Food Systems Transformation Vehicle, hosted on the Joint SDG Fund invest platform, is a blended-finance investment vehicle targeting USD 60M in fund capital to mobilise USD 180M+ in rural investment across Nepal's food systems. It is not a grant call but an investment solicitation offering 4–6% blended returns on a guarantee portfolio and 10–15% IRR on agro-processing PPP investments over a 4–5 year duration. Eligible participants are Development Finance Institutions, impact investors, private equity firms, cooperative banks, and corporate investors. No submission deadline or grant application window is provided. This vehicle is ineligible for ILO participation as it is a private-capital mobilisation mechanism, not a programmatic grant.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the vehicle touches on rural livelihoods, women's economic empowerment, and smallholder agriculture in Nepal, its primary mechanism is private investment mobilisation with financial returns, which is outside the ILO's role as a technical assistance and policy implementing partner.

results.exclusion_reason: Funding objective is not ILO-implementable: the facility is a blended finance investment vehicle targeting private capital mobilisation, SME portfolio returns, and commercial bank partnerships — not a grant for technical assistance or programmatic delivery. Additionally, no grant instrument is present; the funding takes the form of blended/returnable investment with stated fund returns of 3–6%. No open call with a submission deadline exists.
results.classification_summary: The Eastern Europe & Central Asia Green Finance Facility is a blended finance investment vehicle seeking $150M+ to mobilise private capital for green finance and clean energy in the region, with stated financial returns of 3–6% — not a grant. The facility's primary purpose is investment mobilisation and commercial-scale green finance, not technical assistance or programmatic delivery suitable for ILO implementation. No application deadline or open call window is present; the page is a pitch to investors. The ILO is therefore ineligible on multiple grounds: the funding instrument is not a grant, the objective is private capital mobilisation rather than ILO-implementable programmatic work, and there is no active call with a submission deadline.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The page targets investors (DFIs, impact investors, commercial banks, development finance institutions) to co-invest in a blended finance facility. No eligible applicant category includes UN agencies or IGOs as grant recipients; the call is for financial investors, not implementing partners.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The facility explicitly offers blended returns of 3–5% on SME portfolio and 5–6% on household financing, indicating repayable/returnable investment, not a non-reimbursable grant.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No grant amount is specified; the $150M+ figure refers to investment capital with expected financial returns, not a grant.

Deadline is future.evidence: No submission deadline is provided anywhere on the page; there is no application window or deadline to assess.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The facility's central objective is to mobilise $150M+ in blended finance investment and unlock $500M+ in additional commercial bank investments for green finance — a private capital mobilisation vehicle. This is not programmatic technical assistance deliverable by the ILO.

results.title: Eastern Europe & Central Asia Green Finance Facility (GFF)
results.organization: Joint SDG Fund
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies
eligible_activities: Green finance facility replication across 2-4 strategic markets
Affordable green financing for SMEs and underserved households
Renewable energy capacity installation (150MW+)
Coal transition support
Support for women-led enterprises, returning migrants, and marginalised communities
results.proposal_summary: The Eastern Europe & Central Asia Green Finance Facility (GFF), hosted under the Joint SDG Fund's investment platform, seeks to raise $150M+ in blended finance investment to replicate North Macedonia's $46M green finance model across 2–4 markets including potentially Uzbekistan, Kazakhstan, and Kyrgyzstan. The facility targets mobilisation of $500M+ in additional EBRD and commercial bank investments to provide green financing for 100,000+ SMEs and households, install 150MW+ of renewable energy capacity, and support regional coal transition. Current partners include EBRD, Sparkasse Bank, ProCredit Bank, Silk Road Bank, and the Government of North Macedonia. The facility offers blended financial returns (3–6%) over a 4–5 year period and targets development finance institutions, impact investors, and commercial banks as co-investors. No grant instrument, submission deadline, or open call for proposals is present; the page functions as an investor pitch.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the facility mentions support for marginalised communities and women-led enterprises, its primary purpose is private capital mobilisation for green finance and clean energy investment — not labour standards, decent work, social protection, or other core ILO mandate areas.

results.exclusion_reason: Funding objective is not ILO-implementable: this is an investment mobilisation vehicle targeting private capital and commercial co-lending returns, not a grant for programmatic/technical assistance work. Additionally, no grant instrument exists; the fund offers financial returns (5% average) and seeks investors, not grantees. No submission deadline is present.
results.classification_summary: The REIF 2.0 is a blended-finance investment fund seeking to deploy $120M+ and catalyse $480M+ in private commercial investment across the Southern Cone renewable energy sector, offering a 5% average return on a co-lending portfolio. This is an investment mobilisation vehicle, not a grant call, and its primary purpose is to attract capital investors and development finance institutions rather than fund programmatic/technical-assistance work. No grant instrument, no submission deadline, and no open application window are present, triggering multiple hard exclusion criteria.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The page solicits investors (DFIs, commercial banks, corporate impact investors, multilateral funds) to provide capital into a trust fund with financial returns. The ILO is not an eligible applicant type; no entity-type list includes UN agencies or IGOs as grant recipients.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The fund offers a 5% average return on a co-lending portfolio and seeks investors, not grant recipients. This is an investment/co-lending vehicle, not a non-reimbursable grant.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No grant amounts are stated; the fund target of $120M+ refers to investment capital, not grants.

Deadline is future.evidence: No submission deadline is provided anywhere on the page.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page is an investment opportunity pitch inviting interested investors to 'get in touch'; it provides no application form, deadline, or defined submission window. It is effectively a deal-marketing page, not a Call for Proposals.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The primary objective is mobilising private capital and unlocking $480M+ in commercial renewable energy investment through a co-lending trust fund structure with financial returns. This is a private-sector investment mobilisation vehicle, not a technical assistance or programmatic grant that the ILO could implement.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: Not a procurement or tender; it is an investment solicitation.

results.title: Renewable Energy Innovation Fund (REIF) 2.0

eligible_activities: Co-lending for renewable energy businesses

results.proposal_summary: The Renewable Energy Innovation Fund (REIF) 2.0 is a Joint SDG Fund investment vehicle targeting $120M+ in capital to scale Uruguay's existing $50M clean energy trust fund model across the Southern Cone region (Uruguay, Argentina, Bolivia, Colombia and beyond). The fund aims to catalyse $480M+ in additional commercial bank and private investment over a 5–6 year period, offering a 5% average return through a co-lending structure with 20+ commercial bank partners. Eligible activities focus on renewable energy sectors including electromobility, energy storage, waste management, and distributed generation. The fund targets Development Finance Institutions, multilateral banks, regional commercial banks, and corporate impact investors as partners. No grant instrument, submission deadline, or application window is defined; the page invites interested investors to contact the Joint SDG Fund secretariat directly.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The fund peripherally references sustainable employment creation through clean energy transition, but its core purpose is private investment mobilisation in renewable energy, with no alignment to ILO's mandate on labour standards, social protection, or decent work programming.

results.exclusion_reason: Funding instrument is not a grant; funding objective is private capital mobilisation, not ILO-implementable programmatic activity; no open call with a submission deadline.
results.classification_summary: The Zimbabwe Renewable Energy Fund (REF) 2.0 is a private equity investment vehicle seeking to mobilise $100M in capital with a 10–15% IRR, not a grant programme. The primary objective is mobilising private capital and generating financial returns through equity participation in renewable energy enterprises, which is incompatible with ILO's role as a technical assistance implementing partner. No submission deadline or open call for proposals exists; the page solicits investors, not grant applicants. The ILO is therefore ineligible on multiple grounds.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The page targets Development Finance Institutions, private equity investors, pension funds, infrastructure investment funds, and corporate impact investors as potential partners — not UN agencies or intergovernmental organisations as grant recipients. The framing is exclusively investor-oriented.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The facility is a private equity fund promising 10–15% IRR returns under a 'buy-and-hold' model. It explicitly mobilises investment capital, not non-reimbursable grants.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No grant amount is specified; the $100M figure refers to investment capital to be mobilised, not a grant.

Deadline is future.evidence: No submission deadline is provided anywhere on the page. The page is an investor solicitation without a call for proposals or application deadline.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page is an investment opportunity solicitation for a private equity fund, not a call for proposals. There is no application window, submission form, or deadline for grant applicants.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The central objective is mobilising $100M in private equity capital and generating 10–15% IRR through equity participation in renewable energy enterprises — a private capital mobilisation vehicle, not a technical assistance or decent work programme that ILO could implement.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: No procurement or tender language is present; the page solicits private investors.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No co-financing requirement for grant applicants is stated, as this is not a grant call.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No accreditation requirement is mentioned.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned.

results.proposal_summary: The Joint SDG Fund is promoting the Rwanda Cross-Border Digital Trade Platform, an investment mobilisation vehicle targeting $50M in capital (catalysing $200M+ in additional public and private investment) to scale Rwanda's digital cross-border trade infrastructure across border corridors with DRC, Uganda, Tanzania, and Burundi. The platform aims to provide trade financing for 25,000+ traders and SMEs (70% women-led), digitalize trade processes, and create sustainable livelihoods for 100,000+ border community members. results.ilo_match_evidence: While the platform targets women-led SMEs and cross-border traders — populations relevant to ILO's decent work mandate — the vehicle is a private-capital investment fund with IRR targets, not a programmatic grant aligned with ILO's technical assistance and labour standards work.

results.classification_summary: The TriNova 2026 call for triangular cooperation projects explicitly lists eligible applicants as public institutions (national or local), private institutions, civil society, academia, and NGOs — UN agencies and intergovernmental organisations are not included in this exhaustive list, making the ILO ineligible. The page also functions as an aggregator/procedural framework page referencing two annual deadlines (April 30 and October 31), though the attached guidance document does specify a concrete 2026 deadline of May 8, 2026, which has already passed as of today (June 15, 2026). On both the eligibility and deadline grounds, this call must be excluded.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: TriNova contributes up to 300,000 euros per project, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

results.proposal_summary: The TriNova 2026 call, a joint initiative of APC Colombia and Germany's GIZ/BMZ, offers non-reimbursable grants of up to EUR 300,000 per project for triangular cooperation initiatives linking Colombia and a second partner from Latin America, the Caribbean, or the broader Global South, with Germany as facilitating donor. Projects may address any thematic area aligned with the 2030 Agenda and national development plans, and must involve at least three partners (requesting, main, and facilitating), each making a quantified contribution. Eligible applicants include national and local public institutions, private institutions, civil society, academia, and NGOs; UN agencies and IGOs are not explicitly listed as eligible. The implementation period is a maximum of two years. The submission deadline for the 2026 round was May 8, 2026, which has passed. The ILO is not eligible due to its IGO status not being included in the exhaustive eligible applicant categories.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call covers any thematic area including decent work and social protection themes, the eligible applicant list excludes UN/IGO entities such as the ILO, and the triangular cooperation modality is primarily designed for government and civil society partnerships rather than UN implementing agencies.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not among the eligible applicant types listed in the NOFO. The eligible categories are: foreign-based NGOs, U.S.-based NGOs, Public International Organizations, private/public/state institutions of higher education, and for-profit organizations. While 'Public International Organizations' is listed, the ILO's mandate (labour standards, employment, social protection) does not align with this call's primary purpose of documenting and reporting religious freedom abuses in Nigeria. More decisively, the funding objective — accountability for religious freedom abuses — is outside the ILO's mandate and not ILO-implementable.
results.classification_summary: This NOFO from the U.S. Department of State's Office of International Religious Freedom seeks to improve documentation and accountability for religious freedom abuses in Nigeria. Although 'Public International Organizations' is listed among eligible applicants, the call's primary purpose — monitoring, documenting, and reporting religious freedom violations for accountability and advocacy — falls outside the ILO's mandate of labour standards, employment, and social protection. The funding objective is not ILO-implementable, making the ILO ineligible for this opportunity.

This is a human rights/religious freedom documentation program with no connection to labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or other ILO mandate areas.

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Office of International Religious Freedom (IRF) is offering a grant or cooperative agreement of up to USD 3,500,000 to improve documentation, reporting, and accountability for religious freedom abuses in Nigeria. The program targets activities in Nigeria, particularly the Middle Belt region, over a 24–48 month implementation period starting approximately 4 months after award. Eligible applicants include foreign-based and U.S.-based NGOs, Public International Organizations, institutions of higher education, and for-profit organizations; consortia are permitted with a single prime applicant. The call focuses on building civil society capacity to safely document abuses by state and non-state actors (including Boko Haram, ISIS-WA, and Fulani Ethnic Militias), engage accountability mechanisms, and pursue legal remedies. No cost-sharing is required. The application deadline is July 9, 2026.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call focuses exclusively on documenting and reporting religious freedom abuses in Nigeria for accountability purposes, with no connection to the ILO's mandate areas of labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or decent work.

results.exclusion_reason: Deadline has passed: application deadline was March 16, 2026, which is before today's date of June 15, 2026. Additionally, eligible applicant categories are exhaustively listed as U.S.-based non-profits/NGOs, institutions of higher education, for-profit organizations, foreign-based NGOs, and Public International Organizations — while the last category could theoretically include IGOs, the ILO's mandate does not align with providing emergency financial assistance to individual victims of religious persecution.
results.classification_summary: The application deadline of March 16, 2026 has passed, rendering this call ineligible. The call is a U.S. State Department NOFO for a cooperative agreement to provide emergency financial assistance to victims of religious persecution — an objective outside the ILO's mandate. While 'Public International Organizations' are listed among eligible applicants, the ILO match on funding objective is negligible as the primary purpose is direct humanitarian cash assistance to persecuted religious individuals, not labour standards or decent work programming.

This is humanitarian/emergency relief for individuals on religious grounds, not technical assistance, labour standards, social protection systems, or decent work programming that the ILO implements.

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Bureau of Cyberspace and Digital Policy (CDP) offered up to $1.25 million USD in small grants to deploy and integrate advanced digital technologies in Western Hemisphere (WHA) urban systems, targeting Latin America, Central America, South America, and the Caribbean. The program aimed to create economic opportunities for U.S. companies and partner cities by expanding U.S. technology export markets. Eligible activities included IoT, AI, cloud computing, and data analytics projects to make municipal services more efficient and resilient. The deadline for Statements of Interest was March 13, 2026. The ILO, as a UN intergovernmental organization, was not identified as an eligible applicant, and the call's deadline has passed.

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Bureau of Cyberspace and Digital Policy (CDP) launched the Digital Cities Innovation Accelerator Small Grants Program to activate the private sector to deliver innovative solutions to civic challenges in Latin America and the Caribbean through trusted U.S. digital technology. Approximately $1,250,000 USD is available in grants ranging from $100,000 to $700,000 USD each (1–6 awards anticipated), for projects up to 18 months. Eligible applicants include U.S. and foreign-based NGOs, public international organizations, higher education institutions, foreign public entities at the city/municipal level, and for-profit organizations operating in 30 eligible countries across Latin America and the Caribbean. U.S.-based applicants must partner with a local in-country organization; foreign applicants are strongly encouraged but not required to partner with a U.S.-based entity. The two-round application process required a Statement of Interest by March 13, 2026, followed by a full proposal by invitation. Cost share is not required.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call focuses on deploying U.S. digital technology solutions for municipal infrastructure and private-sector development in Latin America, with no alignment to ILO's core mandate of labour standards, social protection, employment policy, or decent work.

eligible_activities: Development and implementation of technologies to circumvent internet censorship

Digital security capacity building for at-risk, vulnerable, and marginalized populations

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights, and Labor (DRL) issued this Request for Statements of Interest in October 2024 to fund Internet Freedom programs globally, with priority in Africa, East Asia and the Pacific, North Africa and the Middle East, South and Central Asia, and the Western Hemisphere. Grant amounts are not specified. Programs were expected to begin no earlier than August/September 2025. Eligible applicants are civil society organizations; UN agencies and intergovernmental organizations are not explicitly included. Applications required a two-step process: first a 3-page Statement of Interest, then a full proposal by invitation. As of June 2026, this call's active window has closed.

Strengthening the diffusion and translation of climate knowledge into public policies and adaptation projects

results.proposal_summary: The AdaptAction programme, co-financed by the European Union and AFD (Agence Française de Développement), is launching a call for action-research projects on climate change adaptation in North Africa and the Middle East, covering Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Egypt, Lebanon, Palestine, Jordan, and Syria. The call aims to fund up to three research projects with a total budget of €310,000 and a maximum of €160,000 per project; projects must begin in Q4 2026 and conclude by 30 April 2028 (maximum 16 months). Eligible applicants are consortia led by a national university or research centre from at least one of the eight programme countries, combined with an internationally referenced institution or researcher; team members may include NGOs, think tanks, consultancies, and individual consultants. The first mandatory stage is the Expression of Interest (AMI), which closes on 3 July 2026; pre-selected applicants will be invited to submit full proposals by 25 September 2026. No co-financing is required and no UN or IGO entity type is listed among eligible applicants.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call is focused on climate change adaptation research (water, agriculture, coastal zones) in the Mediterranean region and does not address labour standards, employment, social protection, decent work, or any other ILO core mandate area.

results.exclusion_reason: Aggregator/index page listing multiple past and current CSO financing mechanisms; no single open call with a specific deadline. Eligible applicants are explicitly CSOs/NGOs, not UN agencies or IGOs.
results.classification_summary: This page is an aggregator/landing page describing multiple AFD CSO financing mechanisms (CSO Initiatives Platform, FISONG, APCC, FSOF, small-scale programs), not a single specific open call for proposals. Additionally, all eligible applicant categories are explicitly limited to CSOs, NGOs, French associations, foundations, and non-profit organizations — UN agencies and intergovernmental organizations are not mentioned as eligible. No specific open application window with a future deadline is identified on the page.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The page explicitly lists eligible applicants as French associations, syndicates, foundations under French law, local CSOs/NGOs, and international non-profit organizations. UN agencies, intergovernmental organizations, and IGOs are not included in the eligible applicant categories.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The CSO Initiatives Platform requires a minimum total project amount of €300,000–€500,000, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: No specific open deadline is provided on this aggregator page. The most recent calls referenced (FISONG 2022, APCC 2022, FSOF) have no future deadlines stated; FISONG was suspended in 2023. No currently open call with a future deadline is identified.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page is an aggregator/index describing multiple financing mechanisms and past calls (FISONG 2018, 2020, 2021, 2022; APCC 2022; FSOF). No single currently-open call with a specific future deadline or application window is presented. FISONG was suspended in 2023.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The funding is explicitly targeted at CSOs/NGOs for international solidarity projects. The ILO, as an IGO, is not an eligible applicant under any of the mechanisms described.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The page states co-financing requirements: AFD provides up to 80% for French CSOs and up to 90% for local CSOs, implying mandatory co-financing of at least 10–20% from the applicant.

results.proposal_summary: This AFD webpage is an aggregator page describing multiple financing mechanisms for CSO/NGO projects, including the CSO Initiatives Platform, Crisis and Post-Crisis calls (APCC), the NGO Sector Innovation Facility (FISONG, now suspended), and the Support Fund for Feminist Organizations (FSOF). The donor is AFD (Agence Française de Développement) supported by the French Ministry for Europe and Foreign Affairs. Eligible applicants are French CSOs/NGOs, foundations, and local civil society organizations registered in DAC-eligible countries. Grant amounts start from €300,000 for the main facility. The page does not represent a single open call and no specific future deadline is provided; the most recent calls referenced are from 2022–2023. The ILO, as a UN intergovernmental organization, is not among the eligible applicant types.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While some thematic areas (social protection, gender equality, crisis response) partially align with ILO's mandate, the funding is exclusively targeted at CSOs and NGOs, making the ILO ineligible as an applicant.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not eligible: the call is explicitly restricted to Swiss companies (private sector). The funding objective is private-sector investment mobilisation, not ILO-implementable programmatic work. Co-financing of at least 50% is mandatory.
results.classification_summary: This call is restricted exclusively to Swiss private-sector companies investing in Ukraine's reconstruction, and the ILO — as a UN intergovernmental organisation — is not an eligible applicant type. The call's primary purpose is mobilising private capital for investment-ready production facilities, which is outside the ILO's mandate as a technical assistance and policy implementing partner. Co-financing of at least 50% is mandatory, and individual grants range from CHF 3 million to CHF 10 million.

results.title: Third Call for Proposals: Private-Sector Investment in Ukraine's Reconstruction (SECO)
results.deadline_text: 20 September
results.organization: Switzerland (State Secretariat for Economic Affairs, SECO)

application_process.description: Call opens; project proposals can be submitted via SECO's Call for Proposals website
Deadline for submission of project proposals
Two-stage selection process; committee of Swiss and Ukrainian authorities decides on support

eligible_activities: Investment projects aimed at building facilities for long-term production in Ukraine
Reconstruction projects in Ukraine implemented in collaboration with the Swiss private sector
funding_restrictions: Applicants must be Swiss companies with an established presence in Ukraine
Companies must contribute at least 50% of total project funding (mandatory co-financing)
Individual project grants range from CHF 3 million to CHF 10 million
results.proposal_summary: SECO (Switzerland's State Secretariat for Economic Affairs) has launched the third call for proposals under its Ukraine country programme 2025–2028, providing up to CHF 50 million in co-financing for Swiss private-sector investment in Ukraine's reconstruction. The call is open from 27 May to 20 September 2026. Eligible applicants are Swiss companies with an established presence in Ukraine; individual grants range from CHF 3 million to CHF 10 million, with companies required to contribute at least 50% of project costs. Supported activities focus on investment in long-term production facilities in Ukraine. Projects are selected via a two-stage process by a joint Swiss-Ukrainian committee. The ILO is not an eligible applicant as the call is exclusively targeting Swiss private-sector companies.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses exclusively on private-sector investment in physical production facilities in Ukraine and is restricted to Swiss companies, with no alignment to ILO's mandate on labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or decent work.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not eligible: the call explicitly restricts applicants to Swiss companies with a presence in Ukraine. The funding objective is private-sector investment mobilisation (construction of production/operational facilities), not ILO-implementable technical assistance or policy work. Co-financing of at least 50% is mandatory.
results.classification_summary: This call is restricted to Swiss private-sector companies with an established presence in Ukraine and therefore excludes the ILO, a UN intergovernmental organisation. The primary objective is co-financing investment projects for constructing production and operational facilities in Ukraine, which is a private-sector investment mobilisation instrument rather than ILO-implementable programmatic work. Mandatory 50% co-financing by applicants further confirms the commercial, private-sector nature of the call.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call explicitly invites 'Swiss companies with a presence in Ukraine' to submit proposals. Eligible applicants are restricted to Swiss private-sector companies; UN agencies, intergovernmental organisations, and IGOs are not listed as eligible entity types.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The federal government provides up to CHF 50 million to co-finance projects, which is a non-reimbursable grant contribution alongside mandatory private co-financing.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Individual project grants range from CHF 3 million to CHF 10 million, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The call is open until 20 September (2026), which is after today's date of 2026-06-15.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The press release dated 28 May 2026 announces the launch of the third call for proposals with a submission deadline of 20 September 2026 and directs applicants to a specific 'Call for Proposals' website.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The call's primary purpose is to mobilise Swiss private capital for investment in construction of production/operational facilities in Ukraine. This is a private-sector investment mobilisation facility, not technical assistance, capacity building, or policy work that the ILO could implement.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a call for project proposals from Swiss companies, not a procurement of goods/services or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Companies are explicitly required to contribute at least 50% of the funding required for their project, making co-financing a mandatory condition.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is mentioned as a requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned; the call targets individual Swiss companies.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Eligibility is restricted to Swiss companies with an established presence in Ukraine, limiting applicants by both nationality (Swiss) and geographic footprint (presence in Ukraine).

results.proposal_summary: Switzerland's State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO) has launched a third call for proposals under its Ukraine Country Programme 2025–2028, offering CHF 3–10 million per project (up to CHF 50 million total) to co-finance investment projects led by Swiss private-sector companies with an established presence in Ukraine. The call focuses on construction and reconstruction of production and operational facilities intended for long-term operation by Ukrainian subsidiaries of Swiss companies, thereby supporting Ukraine's economic reconstruction and resilience. Applicants must be Swiss companies and are required to contribute at least 50% of project costs. Proposals are to be submitted via SECO's dedicated portal by 20 September 2026, with a two-stage selection process running until spring 2027. The ILO is not eligible as only Swiss private-sector companies qualify.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call targets private-sector investment in physical infrastructure in Ukraine with no programmatic focus on labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or other core ILO mandate areas.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not eligible: the call explicitly targets Swiss companies operating in Ukraine. The ILO is a UN intergovernmental organisation, not a company. Additionally, the call's primary purpose is mobilising private-sector reconstruction projects (infrastructure, public transport, health) via Swiss private firms, which is outside ILO's mandate as an implementing partner.
results.classification_summary: The call is restricted to Swiss companies operating in Ukraine, and the ILO as a UN intergovernmental organisation does not fall within the eligible applicant category. The call's primary purpose is to mobilise Swiss private-sector reconstruction projects in Ukraine, which constitutes private-sector investment mobilisation rather than technical assistance or programmatic work the ILO could implement. The deadline of 8 March 2026 has passed relative to today's date of 15 June 2026, providing an additional exclusion ground.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call explicitly invites 'Swiss companies operating in Ukraine' to submit proposals. The eligible applicant category is exhaustively defined as Swiss companies; UN agencies, intergovernmental organisations, or equivalents are not listed.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The federal government is 'contributing' (non-reimbursable) up to CHF 150 million towards projects, framed as a grant/contribution rather than a loan or equity instrument.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Individual grant amounts range from CHF 3 million to CHF 30 million, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The call for proposals was open until 8 March 2026, which has already passed (today is 15 June 2026).

Is open call with active application win.evidence: This page is a press release announcing the second call; the actual call closed on 8 March 2026. The page is a news announcement of a now-closed call, not an active open call.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The call's primary purpose is to mobilise Swiss private-sector companies to deliver reconstruction infrastructure projects in Ukraine (energy, housing, public transport, health). This is private-sector investment mobilisation, not technical assistance or policy work the ILO would implement.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The mechanism is a call for proposals for project grants to Swiss companies, not a procurement/tender for goods or services for SECO itself.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The press release does not specify whether co-financing is mandatory; detailed terms are on a linked page not fully reproduced here.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is mentioned as a requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No information on consortium leadership rules is provided in the press release text.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call is explicitly restricted to Swiss companies operating in Ukraine, meaning applicants must be Swiss-registered private companies.

results.proposal_summary: Switzerland's State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO) launched its second call for proposals on 9 January 2026 to mobilise reconstruction projects in Ukraine in cooperation with Swiss private-sector companies, contributing up to CHF 150 million in total. Individual project grants range from CHF 3 million to CHF 30 million, focusing on Ukraine's key reconstruction needs, particularly public infrastructure including energy, housing, public transport, and health. Only Swiss companies operating in Ukraine are eligible to apply. The two-stage process required submission of project ideas by 8 March 2026, followed by fully developed proposals from selected companies and interviews. The funding falls under Switzerland's Ukraine Country Programme 2025–2028, which has allocated CHF 500 million for Swiss private-sector projects.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call exclusively targets Swiss private companies for infrastructure reconstruction projects in Ukraine, with no alignment with ILO's mandate on labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or decent work programming.
